parsed_findings.chain_steps: Bypass the `sort` allowlist with a valid column and inject SQL through `dir` in the same request.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can alter the query, dump data, or execute destructive SQL depending on the driver.
parsed_findings.proof: `dir=asc DESC; DROP TABLE users;--` reaches `q` unchanged because only `sort` is allowlisted.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a request with a crafted `dir` value and observe the generated SQL includes attacker-controlled text.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `direction` is concatenated into the `ORDER BY` clause without validation, enabling SQL injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use SSRF to hit internal admin or metadata endpoints, then exfiltrate secrets through the proxy response.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can read internal services or metadata and pivot into the private network.
parsed_findings.proof: axios.get(u) uses req.query.url directly, and the only filter is a broken blacklist on line 3.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `/fetch?url=http://127.0.0.1:80/` or a metadata URL and observe the proxy relays the response.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: This server-side fetch proxy accepts attacker-controlled URLs and can reach internal or cloud metadata endpoints.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use traversal to reach sensitive files, then exfiltrate secrets or configs.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can read arbitrary files the process can access.
parsed_findings.proof: `../../etc/passwd` becomes `../etc/passwd`, which resolves above the base directory.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `/download?file=../../etc/passwd` and observe the server try to return a file outside `/srv/data`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Single replace of `../` is bypassable, so `path.join` can still read outside `/srv/data`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Set a malicious `state` cookie, trigger `/resume`, execute code during deserialization, then pivot to full app compromise.
parsed_findings.impact: A remote attacker can execute code in the app process by visiting `/resume` with a forged cookie.
parsed_findings.proof: `pickle.loads()` executes attacker-controlled opcodes during deserialization, and the input comes directly from `request.cookies.get('state')`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a `state` cookie containing a malicious pickle payload; when `/resume` parses it, the payload runs before `obj.get(...)` returns.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted cookie data is base64-decoded and passed to `pickle.loads`, enabling arbitrary code execution via crafted `state` values.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Abuse the update route to overwrite privileged fields, then use the modified account to access restricted actions.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can elevate privileges or alter sensitive account attributes.
parsed_findings.proof: `Object.assign(user, req.body)` copies every client-supplied property onto the persisted object.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a PATCH with fields like `role` or `isAdmin` in the JSON body and observe them saved.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted `req.body` is merged directly into a user record, enabling mass-assignment of protected fields.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the injected shell to read secrets or write a web shell, then pivot to the host.
parsed_findings.impact: Remote code execution as the app user, plus arbitrary file access via `tar` arguments.
parsed_findings.proof: `src` only needs to start with `/var/data/`, so `/var/data/;id` or `name=foo;id` breaks out of `tar`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST JSON like `{"name":"x;id","src":"/var/data/app"}` and observe arbitrary command execution.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `name` and `src` are interpolated into a shell command and executed with `os.system`, enabling command injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the comment endpoint to store script, then trigger it when the comment HTML is displayed.
parsed_findings.impact: Any viewer of comments can be forced to run attacker-controlled script in their browser.
parsed_findings.proof: `markdown.markdown(body)` can emit raw HTML, and `db.insert_comment({'html': rendered, ...})` persists it verbatim.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST a comment like ``; the saved HTML will contain the payload.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted Markdown is converted to HTML and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS if the HTML is later rendered.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Race two requests so both read the same src, then both subtract and credit before either write is observed.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can create money from the same balance and drain accounts via double-spend.
parsed_findings.proof: src is checked at line 6 and both balances are written later without a transaction or lock.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send two concurrent POST /transfer requests for the same user with amounts that each pass the check.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Balance check and updates are non-atomic, so concurrent transfers can overspend the source account.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Forge a token with elevated claims, trigger the production fallback, then reuse the decoded claims as an authenticated session.
parsed_findings.impact: Full auth bypass and privilege escalation if downstream code trusts the returned claims as authenticated identity.
parsed_findings.proof: An attacker can send a tampered JWT that fails `jwt.verify` and still gets decoded and returned in production.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `Authorization: Bearer <forged-jwt>` with a bad RS256 signature; `verify()` throws, then `decode()` returns attacker-controlled claims.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Production falls back to `jwt.decode(t)`, so any invalid or forged token becomes accepted without signature verification.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Login required bypasses anonymous access, but there is no authorization check after parsing `id`.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can enumerate accounts and exfiltrate private user emails and metadata.
parsed_findings.proof: The handler accepts `id`, fetches `users` by that id, and returns the row directly.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Log in, request `/u?id=2`, and observe another user's `id,email` if the account exists.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Any logged-in user can query arbitrary user records by changing `id`, with no ownership or role check.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use timing to learn the valid HMAC, then replay a forged request to reach `process_event(json.loads(body))`.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may forge webhook requests and trigger `process_event()` without the shared secret.
parsed_findings.proof: `got == want` performs a normal string comparison instead of a constant-time check.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send many signed requests with near-matching `X-Sig` values and measure response timing to recover the digest byte by byte.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Using `==` to compare the HMAC leaks timing differences and can let an attacker brute-force the signature.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use a crafted link to `/go?next=//attacker.tld` and capture the victim's browser redirect.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can phish users or bounce them through a trusted origin for credential theft.
parsed_findings.proof: `nxt.startswith('/')` accepts `//evil.com`, and `redirect('//evil.com')` navigates to the attacker host.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: GET `/go?next=//evil.com`; the response redirects the browser to `//evil.com`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Protocol-relative URLs like `//evil.com` pass the slash check and redirect users off-site.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use XXE to exfiltrate sensitive files or pivot to internal services reachable from the app.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can read local files or trigger outbound requests from the server during XML import.
parsed_findings.proof: `etree.XMLParser(resolve_entities=True, no_network=False)` parses `request.data` without disabling entity expansion or external fetches.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST XML with a `<!DOCTYPE>` that defines an external entity and read it via `<title>&xxe;</title>`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The parser resolves external entities and allows network access, enabling XXE on attacker-controlled XML.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Send crafted JSON body, exploit selector injection in `findOne`, then authenticate without valid credentials.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may log in as another user or bypass credential checks entirely.
parsed_findings.proof: `q` uses `req.body.email` and `req.body.password` without type checks or escaping before `db.users.findOne(q)`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Submit `email` or `password` as an object like `{"$ne":null}` and observe authentication bypass if the DB accepts operator syntax.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `req.body` is passed directly into the database selector, enabling NoSQL/operator injection if objects reach `findOne`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Overflow `buf` to corrupt the saved return address and redirect execution.
parsed_findings.impact: This can crash the program or let an attacker overwrite control data on the stack.
parsed_findings.proof: An input longer than 63 bytes will overflow `buf` because `gets` never bounds the read.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Run the program and paste more than 63 non-NUL bytes at the prompt.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `gets(buf)` reads unbounded input into a 64-byte stack buffer, enabling a stack overflow.

parsed_findings.impact: Full confidentiality loss for every encrypted payload.
parsed_findings.proof: KEY is a literal constant at line 2 and is reused for every call to enc().
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Read the source, extract KEY, and decrypt any blob produced by this function.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The AES key is hardcoded, so anyone who gets the code can decrypt all ciphertexts.

parsed_findings.impact: Integrity loss and possible privilege or data manipulation if the plaintext is consumed.
parsed_findings.proof: The function returns IV plus raw CBC ciphertext and does not add any MAC or AEAD tag.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Flip bits in a ciphertext block and decrypt to observe controlled plaintext corruption.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: CBC encryption is unauthenticated, so attackers can tamper with ciphertext and change plaintext.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Pass `out=NULL` with any positive `out_sz`, then the function dereferences it at line 5.
parsed_findings.impact: A caller-controlled NULL destination causes a denial of service.
parsed_findings.proof: With `out == NULL` and `out_sz > 0`, line 3 does not reject the call and line 5 writes through `out`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `copy_name("x", NULL, 2)`; it reaches `memcpy(out, in, n)` and crashes.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The null-check is inverted, so a NULL `out` is accepted and then dereferenced in `memcpy`.
